sentences.text: My name is Howard Chandler.
My original name was Hy Wajchendler.
I was born and raised in a small town in central Poland in the province of Kielce by the name of Wierzbnik,Wie now known as Starachowice.
I was born in 1928, December 5, 1928.
It was a small town, relatively speaking.
It had big ammunition factories in those days even before the war, long before the war.
And the Jewish population, I would-- I would imagine was around 3,000 people.

The general population must have been around 20,000 people, and they were more or less scattered in the outlying areas of town, because the center of the town was mostly occupied mostly by Jewish people.
Of course, there were Gentile people there, as well, but mostly around the market square, where business took place.

There was a market there on Thursdays and the peasants and the farmers of the surrounding area used to bring their produce and their fruit and it was a-- and merchants used to come display their wares.
It was quite a day to look forward to.
My father had a store and he also had a spot in the marketplace, where we would go on Thursday.
He would take our ware out there, display them in this designated square-- and he used to sell kitchenware there, pots, pans, glasses, china, things like this.
On Wednesday night, we packed the wagon, which I was a helper, as well, as well as the other children.
And this was Thursday.
The Jewish population there, I guess, as they were throughout Poland, mostly religious people.
They were observant Jews.
The Sabbath was kept strictly, so were the other holidays, of course.
Jewish children mostly went to Jewish school, or cheder, and we also attended the general public schools in town, of which they were about three, I think.
We attended the school like my brothers, and sister, myself, we attended the school nearest to our house, which was not too far away, up the hill, across from the church.
And there, I went to school until the war broke out.
I finished three years of public school.
I passed from the third to fourth grade, and I was never allowed to go back to school because of the German occupation.
The Christian children were also not allowed to go back to school.
I don't know whether it was policy.
The German soldiers occupied the school as their quarters, but they moved out and the Christian children were allowed to go back to school, but not the Jewish children.
Of course.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My family consisted of my parents.
Leibke Wajchendler, and my mother's name was Pearl Blima.
And I had an older sister, and an older brother.
My older sister's name was Gitel, or Gutcha.
My brother's name is Harry, or Hershel.
And then I was in the middle of three brothers.
I was called Chamu, or Chaim.
And I had a younger brother, Ishmuel, Ishmulek.
Of all the family, besides my parents, I kind of-- when I think back of my family, I think back with a tear of my eye, for my smaller brother.
I must have been quite close to him.
And we attended-- my older brother went to a different cheder, and my brother and I, oldest, went to the same teacher of the same cheder.
And, you know, it's tragic what happened to him.
It's tragic what happened to everybody, but it touches me when I think of him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father was involved, although he was a businessman-- he was friends with the people in town who had mostly to do with the courts.
I'm not sure until today, whether he had a license to practice law or he studied law and he didn't finish school.
So the judges and the lawyers and the official in the court were his friends.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] whether he was clever enough, or he had the knowledge, I don't know, that he predicted exactly what it is going to happen to the Jews, and he made arrangements for everybody in the family to be situated in such a state where we will be saved from expulsion, from being sent away to other places.
Like he knew that other places are being-- that the Jewish people are being rounded up and taken away to farms in the Ukraine, or whatever else.
The Germans were saying that they were taking the Jews away.
So he arranged for me and my brother-- to the front of his-- Jozef Kowacki, he was a famous lawyer in town.
He had a big [NON-ENGLISH] or farm near [NON-ENGLISH] called [NON-ENGLISH].
And he was supposed to hide us out there.
What year was this, do you know?
This was in 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He also made arrangements for all the family to obtain work permits.
We had ammunition factories in town, although Jews were not permitted to work there before the war.
During the war after the occupation, the Germans allowed Jews to be employed there.
So my father got a job in these factories and so did my older brother.
He also obtained permits to work in these factories for my mother, my older sister, and of course, my older brother.
And then I was in the verge, I was, in 1942, I must have been around 11 years old-- no, I was 11, almost 13 years old then, in 1942.
I was 11 when the war broke out.
And I also had a work permit.
Now, my younger brother, we come back to my younger brother.
He must have been about 11 then.
And he was so smart in school, in cheder-- you know, we used to go to the [NON-ENGLISH] to the weekly portion of the-- of the-- of the Bible to go through, learn it.
And came around Thursday or Friday time, the teacher used to test you, how well you know.
When it came Friday, I wasn't that good, but my younger brother, he only had to study Monday and Tuesday and he knew it by heart everything else.
So he was very smart.
I would say he was much smarter than me.
At the day-- at the day of the expulsion, he arranged-- not the day of the expulsion-- he arranged before for a Christian family, who were neighbors and old family friends, they should hide him out.
And it was agreed amongst all of us, we had work permits.
Either Mr.
Kowacki-- who was supposed to come and take my brother and myself away on a Thursday-- the expulsion took place on a Tuesday.
So we were stuck.
So I used my work permit.
My brother went to this Christian family at the day of the expulsion, which took place on the 27th of October, 1942.
Please explain for those of us who don't know what the expulsion meant.
Well, I was a small boy, about 11.
I didn't know too much what was taking place.
What did-- what did take place is, we lived in the town.
Under German occupation at the beginning, it was no difference to us, except I was not allowed to go back to school, and that they burned the synagogue down.
I should like to come back to that.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We learned, or we heard-- of course, people were being rounded up in town, and taken to forced labor.
Some returned and some didn't return, so we didn't know where they were being taken.
And of course, nobody liked to lose either a son or a brother or a father to be taken away to a strange place and to be worked to death.
So nobody wanted to go away and everybody tried to be protected.
But on the other hand, we heard what was taking place in other locations, in other cities.
Of course, we had no radio.
We had no newspapers.
Radio was forbidden to have under penalty of instant death.
They shot you once they found that you were listening to any broadcast.
So the only news we used to get was from people who used to go away from other place, and they used to go to town.
And they would visit either friends that they knew or related to somebody.
And then you heard stories what was taking place in other towns.
And what I could hear the older people talk about, that they were telling stories that were hard to believe.
Pardon?
That the people are being rounded up, put on trains, and being sent away.
And some locations where the population was greater than maybe a train could accommodate, they used to-- they might have maybe one or two evacuations like this.
They were called [SPEAKING POLISH], or in German Aussiedlung.
And they were compared to what you might call an expulsion.
Where the Jewish population was ordered to assemble, in most places in the market square.

And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we were told, or we were made to believe, that we were being taken to the Ukraine, where Ukrainian farmers were abandoning their farm, and they needed people to work these farms.
That's where we were being taken.
And I don't know whether people believe this or didn't believe it.
They allowed you to take a certain weight in luggage, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that was it.
The end result is that they rounded up people, they marched them down to the trains-- in our place, maybe I'm going too much ahead of myself.
I don't know.
But up until October 1942, the Jewish people were allowed to live, prior to that, where they lived before.
But in 1941, 1940, 1941, they established a ghetto.
That means that people in certain streets had to move out into other streets where the ghetto was established.
Consequently, people who lived in the places where they we were not expelled, like made to move, had to share their accommodation with other people from town.

Besides this, in 1940, I believe, they brought in a trainload of people from the city of Plotzk, and a train load of people in the city of Lodz, or Lodz-- left them on the street-- on the train, and the Judenrat-- which was then established-- had to invite these people into the town.
And every family had to share their accommodation, and since these people came with nothing, no luggage at all.
So every household was now overcrowded.
Food was getting scarce.
Came the wintertime, you couldn't buy any fuel for the-- for the heating the houses, so it was cold.
Epidemics broke out, although we had doctors-- a couple of Jewish doctors.
They had no medicine and people just died needlessly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And as time progressed, it was getting worse and worse.
In our household, we-- we-- we had a mother and two sons from Lodz.
We had a mother and two daughters and a son from the city of Plotzk.
We gave up our store to a friend of my father's by the name of Lustgarten, he was a tailor, and he was making suits for the Gestapo.
He was a man about my stature, and I remember some Gestapo people came there to measure suits-- he had to stand up on a chair, they were so big in relation to him.
But he was a master craftsman.
And this is-- and they had their favorite shoemakers, and they had their favorite tailors, they had their favorite watch makers.
And they held these people high regards, because they were tradesmen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Our house was happened to be in the place where the ghetto was established.
My personal friends were mostly non-Jews.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were made to wear-- to wear the armbands, the white armband with the white star of David on it so as to distinguish you between the Jew and the Catholic-- because either there were Jews or there were Catholics, Gentile or Jews.
And I hardly wore this-- only when my parents didn't see me.
I used to go out of the house, take the thing off, and join my friends and go in the fields, you know?
We raided the priest's orchard, and other orchards came this time of year, particularly.
What would happen if you were caught without it?
Well, they would shoot you.
But either I-- I was too stupid to take any notice of it, or I had no fear.
I don't remember.
But I wanted to be with my friends, so this is what I did.
I also stepped out of the ghetto, and I visited the village from where my grandfather came from.
My mother took me there once, particularly, when the food started to run out, and you couldn't obtain any more food.
And my grandfather, apparently, must have been well known in this village.
Now, to get there, you had to go to the end of town and cross the forest.
And there was a slight distinguishable path.
She took me there once, and she showed me the village.
And we went back.
And this is the chance that she took once to go out without a armband.
Subsequent to this, my older brother and I used to go there on a regular basis, at least once a week.
We would go to the village, and we we would knock on the farmer's door and we would say, we are the grandsons of Mordko Sztarkman.
He must have been very well known in this village.
And once we mentioned his name, every door was opened.
And we would say that we are running short of food, could they spare any food?
And most of the time, we came back either with some potatoes, and if the fruit was on the trees, they would let us take some fruit down.
And sometimes they used to take to them a bar of soap, or some salt that we still had leftover from the stock from the store, and we would exchange for flour or anything like this.
And we would carry it back.
And this helped us out.
And of course, we had to share everything with these other families who had been so-called-- so invited to our house.
There was no such thing as privacy.
You couldn't eat on your own if you wanted to, you had to share everything.
We had a lot of Gentile friends, customers in the store.

And people who were supposed to hide out my brother, we gave them most of our possessions knowing that we wouldn't-- when the expulsion takes place, you will not-- we would not be able to take it with us.
So we gave them money, we gave them shirts, clothing, whatever we had, we gave it to them.
And this is what hurts me so much.

When the day of the expulsion took place, which is every time I think of this, and I close my eyes, you know, I still shudder-- the such brutality that this took place, although we expected it more or less.
You would see people being shot-- and these are not strange people.
Most people knew each other.
You tried to go and help someone who was beaten up or shot, they would shoot you too, and beat you up.
So you could see-- you could see scenes under the people that haven't been there cannot imagine what took place.
If anybody can see a civilian population being forced out of their houses.
They weren't able to take any possessions that was dear to them.
You have people-- old people who cannot walk, or crippled people who aren't as fast to obey the commands from the soldiers.
I still get nightmares from it.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although the Jewish population before the war was about 3,000, at the day of the expulsion, I would imagine it must have been at least between 8,000 and 10,000 people, because not only the two trainloads of people who came from Lodz and Plotzk, the people from the adjoining villages were expelled well prior to that, and they also came to live in the-- in the town of Wierzbnik.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] At the day of the expulsion, we went out, because we lived only two houses away from the market square, so it wasn't far to go.
I was on the way to work already in the morning, and I could see a lot of strange soldiers.
You get to know the occupation soldiers-- you know, you got to know the gendarmes, and the Ukrainians who were the guards at the factory.
You get to see different uniforms, different soldiers, rounding up the people.
I went back instead of going to work, and I alarmed, like, my family.
So we were prepared more or less for it.
I had a special suit made from strong material, which should last long.
I put on two or three shirts, in case my rucksack would be taken away, so at least I would be warm.
And we marched out to this expulsion, besides seeing all the brutalities on the way.
Many people were shot, many people were beaten up.
Anyways, just bedlam, you know, everybody trying to stay together with their families, more or less.
And the market square was full of people.
Although we were one of the first ones to go there.
As the day progressed, we went out to market square.
My brother went to these Christian people, by the name of Glina, on the same street we lived, except it was further down nearly out of town.
And he went there, it was pre-arranged.
And we stayed in the market square, and people were coming out.

When the people were full, when the market square was full already, and we were surrounded by all these soldiers, the fellow in charge of the employment in this-- in these Hermann Goering worker, and these ammunition factories-- barked out an order to all those who are holding work permits should step out.
So my father stepped out, I stepped out, my brother wasn't there, because he was working the night shift.
So he was in the factories, and they didn't-- they weren't allowed to go back home.
They kept them there for the day of expulsion.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Now, this was just to illustrate a quirk of fate.
I had a rucksack with me, and I stepped out together with my father, and we were on the way.
He said all those workers have to assemble on the sidewalk, against the houses at the end of the square.
So the fellow from the [NON-ENGLISH], from the employment, stopped me on the way there.
He stopped me and he says to me, how old are you?
I said, I'm 16 years old.
I knew him by the name, and I called him Herr Schwertner-- he was in civilian clothes.
He wasn't a soldier.
He was a German official in charge of the employment in these factories.
And he didn't know what to do with me.
So one of the Jewish officials from the Judenrat went by, and his name was Moishe Berencwajg, and he says, Berencwajg, come over here.
Berencwajg came over.
He says, [NON-ENGLISH].
So he comes over to me, and he says to me, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
He's 16 years old, and he's a good worker.
And he let me go.
I assembled with all the workers.
And it happened to be, I was standing near a gate that led into a court yard.
And other people are stepping out-- the ladies separate from the men, and we were thinking my mother, my sister, they have work cards and they will be assembled with the ladies there.
And as soon as we were assembled, I could see that my friends who were also my age, who also had work permits, they were coming out.
Show me the work permit, and they wanted to go through.
He used to go over.
As soon as he's shown the work permit, he take the work permit, tear it up, and direct them back to join the people in the square.
And then he came looking for me.
He remembered he let me go by.
And I was observing him.
And every time he went by, I would slip into this gate, and he wouldn't see me.
Until we marched away.
My friends never made it.
They were sent together, with all the population.
They were marched down to the train, never to be seen or heard from again.
We were marched to the factories where nearby they had established three forced labor camps, and we were accommodated there.
There was the [NON-ENGLISH], and there was the [NON-ENGLISH], and there was a [NON-ENGLISH].
So some people worked on the [NON-ENGLISH], which was planing mill.
They were making wood products, mainly ammunition boxes.
And we worked in the ammunition factories.
We were making, out of iron ore and scrap metal-- we were making steel for ammunition.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were accommodated in these three camps and you had to get used to do life in the camp, of course.
As soon as I got to the camp-- no, before we got to the camp, we were assembled in a place called [NON-ENGLISH], which means a rifle range.
There were two banks of earth on both sides, and on the front was the rifle range.
And the fellow by the name of-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forget his name, it'll come back to me-- made a speech, an SS man.
I guess he was an SS man, or a state man in black uniform, that we should surrender all valuables-- money, watches, jewelery, foreign currencies, whatever we had of value to.
And there was a huge box there.
And he made the people go through and throw everything that was of value there.
As soon as about 20 people went by, he stopped about three or four people and he searched them to see, make sure that everything was put in.
And if he found anything on somebody, he made them wait there and another few people went by and he stopped another few people.
Then he stopped about five or eight people who didn't throw everything in.
He ordered two or three soldiers to come.
He'd put them up against the wall and shot them right in front of everybody there.
So everybody watching this made quite sure that they surrendered everything.
Of course, when my turn came, I put in-- I had some money.
My parents divided-- every child should have some money on them, and I threw in everything that I had.
From there, they marched us to camp.
There were some other incidents there that-- see, I was one of the smallest there.
They took me to my place of work-- my shift was from the first shift.
This was late in the afternoon already.
The shift was finished.
So we went from [NON-ENGLISH] the [NON-ENGLISH], to the [NON-ENGLISH] where they made raw steel.
And they marched out the other Jewish people who were there from the first shift to walk towards camp.
The Ukrainians picked me out, took me into the guard house, and they gave me a thorough search.
And they think I might have some valuables on me.
Of course, they didn't find anything.
What happened?
The group had left already.
They searched me.
When I come out, there was nobody there, and I had to run to catch up with the column of people.
And there was a Jew on the loose, easy picking for anybody with a gun.
I still remember this.
I had to cross the railway tracks.
There was a train coming, I had to wait until the train goes by and when I reached the column, I couldn't-- I couldn't run anymore.
So they helped me out and we walked to the camp, which was situated on top of a cliff of a stone quarry and we had to-- we took a back road or something.
We had to scale the quarry to go to camp.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I think I missed telling you what happened to my brother.
My brother had gone to these Christian people.
As the expulsion was taking place, he came-- he came to the market square.
I know he wasn't stupid.
He was smart enough to realize that he was supposed to stay with these Christian people.
He came, and of course, he was expelled with everybody else.
My mother's work permit wasn't honored, and nor was my sister's.
So my sister, my mother, and my younger brother, together with the whole of the Jewish population, was done away with at that particular day.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father, my older brother, myself-- because we were encamped in this forced labor camp-- and we stayed there under terrible conditions.
The first few days, when I run back to the column after these Ukrainian guards were searching me, my father went to work and I was assigned a barrack.
I remember just like now.
The-- the bunks were so shallow that if you slipped in on your stomach, you couldn't turn around.
You had to come out and go in on your back.
And I was there for about three days, crying, until my father finally found me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And what happened when he found me is, I-- the bunks were bedded out with sawdust.
There was no straw like for mattresses.
It was boards with sawdust.
He took me out of the barrack.
He says, you come and join me in my barrack.
Fine.
So I says, well, before we go in your barrack, I says, I got lots of sawdust in my shirt.
I shake it out.
I took off my shirt.
It was full of lice.
So I says, OK, let's throw it away.
So my father says, he says-- there's no mother here.
You don't throw things away, because nobody is here is going to give you another shirt.
So he took me to the barrack of his.
And the next day, I joined him on his-- on his workstation.
And he worked in a smelter, where they were melting iron ore and scrap metal, making steel for the real heavy field guns.
They put in a-- they stuck in a piece of steel into the oven, took it out, stuck in a bucket of water, put the shirt in, and that was how you did your laundry.
